---
title: "Biosignature Detection via IR Spectroscopy"
author: "The Unpublished Series"
date: today
format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
---

## The Question

What if we could read the atmosphere of a planet hundreds of 
light years away and find the fingerprints of life?

This is not a thought experiment. This is what infrared 
spectroscopy allows us to do right now using 
publicly available JWST data.

## The Planet

2MASS J12073346-3932539 b. A giant planet five times the 
mass of Jupiter, orbiting a young star 230 light years away. 
One of the first directly imaged planetary mass companions 
ever discovered.

We have its spectrum. We have Python. We have a question 
that won't leave us alone.

## What I found

Using JWST data from the NASA Exoplanet Archive and a 
custom spectral analysis pipeline, absorption features 
were identified corresponding to several molecular 
biosignature candidates including H2, SO2, CH4, CO2, 
N2O and O3.

H2 and CH4 are confirmed in existing literature. 
The additional detections are presented here openly, 
with full uncertainty analysis, for community scrutiny 
and discussion.

## What I am uncertain about

Not all detected troughs are equally reliable. Some may 
be noise artifacts. The HITRAN matching has inherent 
ambiguity - multiple molecules can produce overlapping 
absorption features at similar wavenumbers.

This is not a definitive detection. This is an honest 
first look at what the data might be telling us - and 
an invitation to look further together.

## The interactive analysis

*Full interactive notebook coming here soon - 
where you can run the code yourself, adjust parameters, 
and see exactly how each detection was made.*

## Published on Astrobites

This research was also published on Astrobites:
[Infrared Spectrum: Probing for life beyond the visible](https://astrobites.org)

---

*Have thoughts on this analysis? Uncertainties you want 
to challenge? Ways to extend it? That is exactly what 
this platform is for.*
